coredata.dc:title: Controllable functionalization of carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for independent Cr(Ⅵ), Fe(Ⅲ) and Cu(Ⅱ) ions detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Xiaochun
Ren, Shoutian
Wang, Linlin
Gai, Qixiao
Dong, Quanli
Liu, Wenjun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensors have been successfully used for heavy metal ion detection in drinking water, but the following two points hinder their large scale applications: (1) CDs usually exhibit poor response selectivity for specific ions; (2) there is a lack of multifunctional CDs probes with independent detection of multiple ions. So it’s very urgent to develop multifunctional CDs fluorescent probes for their independent and highly selective detection of multiple ions in water. In this work, carbon nanosheets (CNs) were first stripped from carbon rods by the electrochemical method, and then polymerized into CDs less than 10 nm in size and simultaneously functionalized with ammonia, N,N-Dimethylformamide or polyethylene glycol in the hydrothermal/solvothermal process, respectively. These functionalization can selectively introduce CN bonds, NH2 and OH groups on CDs surface, respectively. These three functionalized CDs exhibit evidently enhanced fluorescence response to Cr6+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively. Compared with the bare CNs, their sensitivity and selectivity can be enhanced by more than 12 and 7 times, respectively. Their limit of detection is reduced by at least one order of magnitude, and the linear response intervals are significantly broadened to low-concentration region (i.e., 0.8–20.0 μM).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensors
Multifunctional CDs probes
Functionalization
Ions detection
Functional groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of functionalized CDs and their size distribution: (a) and (b) af-CDs, (c) and (d) df-CDs, (e) and (f) pf-CDs, respectively. The size histograms in panel (a), (c) and (e) are the size distribution of af-, df- and pf-CDs, respectively.
FT-IR spectra of CNs, af-, df- and pf-CDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescent spectra (b) of CNs, af-, df- and pf-CDs.

Fluorescence response of CNs to 0-100 μM Cu2+ ions (a) and its relationship of fluorescence intensity and Cu2+ concentration (b). Fluorescence response of af-CDs to 0-100 μM Cr6+ ions (c) and its relationship of fluorescence intensity and Cr6+ concentration (d).

Fluorescence response of four probes to different cations: (a) CNs, (b) af-CDs, (c) df-CDs and (d) pf-CDs. Sensitivity is defined as S=
F 0
- F
F 0
* 100 % , and F0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of CDs and that in presence of 80 μM cations, respectively.
Fluorescence response of af-CDs to Cr6+ as a function of reaction time (a), in the presence of 10−5-1 M NaCl (b) and in different pH environments (c).
A schematic illustration of the fluorescence response of three functionalized CDs.
Response parameters of four probes to specific cations.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: (Sd-Sr)/Sr is defined to describe the selectivity of CDs probes, where Sd and Sr represent their sensitivity to the detection ion and strongest reference ion, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Parameter summary of passivated/functionalized CDs sensors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Symbol “*” indicates the values are not explicitly stated in the articles, but approximated from a graphical figure in the references.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent response results of af-CDs, df-CDs and pf-CDs in lake water samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The measured concentration of Cr6+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions is obtained according to the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and added ions concentrations (Figs. 4 and Fig. S4). Recovery (%) is defined as the ratio of the measured and added concentration.

head.ce:title: Controllable functionalization of carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for independent Cr(Ⅵ), Fe(Ⅲ) and Cu(Ⅱ) ions detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Optoelectronic Science, Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai, Weihai 264209, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Optoelectronic Science, Harbin Institute of Technology at Weihai, Weihai 264209, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Continuous Education College, Ludong University, Yantai 264205, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Continuous education college, Ludong University, Yantai, 264205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fluorescence response of three functionalized CDs.

Carbon-dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensors have been successfully used for heavy metal ion detection in drinking water, but the following two points hinder their large scale applications: (1) CDs usually exhibit poor response selectivity for specific ions; (2) there is a lack of multifunctional CDs probes with independent detection of multiple ions. So it’s very urgent to develop multifunctional CDs fluorescent probes for their independent and highly selective detection of multiple ions in water. In this work, carbon nanosheets (CNs) were first stripped from carbon rods by the electrochemical method, and then polymerized into CDs less than 10 nm in size and simultaneously functionalized with ammonia, N,N-Dimethylformamide or polyethylene glycol in the hydrothermal/solvothermal process, respectively. These functionalization can selectively introduce CN bonds, NH2 and OH groups on CDs surface, respectively. These three functionalized CDs exhibit evidently enhanced fluorescence response to Cr6+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively. Compared with the bare CNs, their sensitivity and selectivity can be enhanced by more than 12 and 7 times, respectively. Their limit of detection is reduced by at least one order of magnitude, and the linear response intervals are significantly broadened to low-concentration region (i.e., 0.8–20.0 μM).

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of metallurgy, papermaking, and textile industries, heavy metal pollution has become serious economic and social problems. For example, abundant ions, such as chromium ions (Cr6+), copper ions (Cu2+) and iron ions (Fe3+), are discharged into the drinking water, and gradually enriched in human tissues and organs. These ions enrichment can cause many serious diseases: excess Cr6+ can injure the kidneys and liver [1]; excess Cu2+ ingestion can result in headaches, dizziness, Wilson and Menkes diseases [2–4]; excess Fe3+ leads to slow metabolism and Alzheimer's disease [5,6]. So their concentrations in drinking water are strictly limited to be 5.4 μM, 15.6 μM and 1.0 μM by Ministry of Health of China (GB-5749−2005), respectively. Therefore, it’s considerably urgent to exploit a simple and efficient method to detect heavy metal ions in water, and fluorescence quenching can perfectly meet the above requirements [7]. Carbon dots (CDs) are popular fluorescence probes due to their tunable surface functionalities, strong fluorescence, good water solubility, low toxicity and high photostability [8–13].

For CDs probes, they are usually decorated with abundant functional groups (i.e., hydroxyl group, carboxylic group and amino group), leading to their simultaneous response to multiple ions and the corresponding poor response selectivity [14–16]. Meanwhile, different functional groups exhibit significantly selective binding with specific ions. For example, the hydroxyl group exhibits the selective and strong coordination interaction with Fe3+ ions [17]. The carboxylic group exhibits excellent affinity with Hg2+ ions [18], and the amino group is inclined to form complex with Cu2+ ions [2]. So the selective introduction of specific functional groups by surface passivation/functionalization can enhance the fluorescence response, especially the response selectivity of CDs [19].

Concerning the functionalization treatment of CDs, it’s usually in situ implemented during their synthesis process by adjusting experimental conditions (i.e., solvent, precursor, pH and so on) [20], so the precise controlling of their functional groups is relatively difficult. By contrast, the post-functionalization can initiatively and selectively introduce specific functional groups for specific ion detection [21]. Meanwhile, multiple ions are usually required to be simultaneously detected in drinking water, so multifunctional fluorescent sensors are pursued for their independent detection. At present, there are few reports on CDs-based multifunctional fluorescent sensors [9,22]. In this article, post-functionalization strategies were introduced to construct multifunctional CDs fluorescent probes for independent detection of three ions in water.

All chemicals of analytical grade were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Shanghai, China, which included ammonia, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), polyethylene glycol (PEG-600), l-ascorbic acid (AA), Glutathione (GSH), K2Cr2O7, AlCl3·6H2O, LiOH·6H2O, Mg(OH)2, Mn(CH3COO)2, KNO3, Sb(CH3COO)3, 3CdSO4·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, HgCl2 and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O. Deionized (DI) water was used in the whole experiment.

CNs were stripped by the electrochemical method described in a previous reference [23]. In detail, two clean carbon rods were placed in parallel with the distance of 7.5 cm in 600 mL of DI water, acting as the anode and cathode, respectively, with static potential of 15−60 V supplied by a direct current power. After continuous stripping for 120 h, the solution gradually changed from colorless to dark yellow, indicating the successful stripping of CNs. After eliminating large particles with filter paper (0.22 μm) and centrifugation (15,000 rpm) for 30 min, the CNs solution was obtained for the further functionalization.

Three schemes were used to synthesize and functionalize CDs by the hydrothermal/solvothermal method: (1) 17 mL of CNs solution was mixed with 3 mL of ammonia, and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then excess ammonia in the reaction solution was completely evaporated at 80 °C for 1 h, these ammonia functionalized CDs were marked as af-CDs; (2) 2.5 mg of CNs was dispersed in 9 g of DMF and heated at 160 °C for 6 h in the same autoclave, and the functionalized CDs were marked as df-CDs; (3) 0.5 g of PEG-600 was ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL of CNs solution for 20 min and heated at 120 °C for 12 h, and the functionalized CDs were marked as pf-CDs. These three CDs were thoroughly cleaned with ethanol and DI water, respectively.

The morphologies and crystalline structure of CDs were acquired by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX-2700) with Cu kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) as radiation source. Their infrared and absorption spectra were acquired on Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, NICOLET 380) at 500-4000 cm−1 and UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV1800, SHIMADU), respectively. Their zeta potential was measured by ZETASIZER 3000 HSA system. The excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of three functionalized CDs were obtained by fluorescence spectrometer (F-2700, Hitachi). Two fluorescence spectrometers (Suzhou Winray Optoelectronic Technology Co, Ltd) were used to measure the fluorescence spectra of CDs with 365 nm LED (10 mW) and 405 nm laser (70 mW) as excitation source for ions detection, respectively, and marked as FS-365 and -405 for simple description.

To estimate the fluorescence response of these CDs to multiple ions, a certain amount of diluted CDs solution and metal ions (i.e., Cr6+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) were mixed in a quartz cuvette of 4 mL, and appropriate fluorescence spectrometers was selected according to their optical absorption features. FS-365 was used to estimate the response of af- and df-CDs, and FS-405 for CNs and pf-CDs, respectively. Their response to other reference ions, namely Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Li+, K+, Sb3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, was used to estimate their selectivity performance. To assess their anti-interference ability, the effect of 80 μM interfering ions on the fluorescence response of CDs was measured. A certain amount of reducing agents (AA for af- and pf-CDs, GSH for df-CDs) were used to estimate the reversibility of quenched CDs.

The QYs of CDs were measured with quinine sulfate as standard reference according to the following equation [24,25]:
Ф = Ф ' ×
A '
I '
×
I A
×
(
n
n '
)
2
Where Ф, A and I represent the QY, optical absorption intensity and integrated fluorescence intensity of CDs, respectively. The parameters marked with apostrophe were used to describe the corresponding ones of quinine sulfate. n and n′ are the refractive indexes of CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. The diluted CDs and quinine sulfate solutions were prepared to ensure their light absorbance intensity less than 0.1 at the excitation region. The QYs of af-CDs and df-CDs were measured by FS-365 to be 2.3 % and 3.5 %, respectively. The QYs of CNs and pf-CDs were measured by FS-405 to be 0.5 % and 1.1 %, respectively.

The lake water was collected from Sun Moon Lake at HIT (Weihai) campus to estimate the fluorescence response of functionalized CDs in real samples. The lake water was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and the clean lake water was used to prepare the ions solution to be tested.

As shown in Fig. S1, two-dimensional CNs are successfully prepared by the electrochemical stripping method. Their partial enlargement image exhibits the lattice spacing of 0.330 nm, corresponding to the interlayer distance of graphene. The XRD patterns of CNs and corresponding glass substrate are shown in Fig. S2, and no obvious diffraction peaks are observed, which may be due to its too small loading. As shown in Fig. 1 , after the hydrothermal treatment with ammonia, DMF and PEG-600, respectively, CNs are successfully transformed into CDs, maybe due to their thermal agglomeration under high-temperature and high-pressure hydrothermal conditions. These CDs exhibit relatively narrow size distribution with the average of 6.6, 6.1 and 5.7 nm for af-, df-, and pf-CDs, respectively. Their identical lattice spacing of 0.237 nm corresponds to the diffraction plane (102) of graphitic carbon [26].

FT-IR spectra of CNs and functionalized CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . For CNs, their peaks at 1250, 1340, 1410 and 1570 cm−1 correspond to CO bonds, bending vibration of CH bonds, COO groups, and NH bending vibration of amino groups, respectively [27–30]. The peaks at 3060 and 3150 cm−1 correspond to aromatic CH stretching vibration [31]. For af-CDs, the appearance of the peak at 1060 cm−1 indicates the introduction of CN bonds during hydrothermal ammonia treatment process, which can efficiently enhance the fluorescence response of CDs to Cr6+ [32,33]. For df-CDs, the enhanced peak at 1570 cm−1 confirms the introduction of more NH2 groups by DMF solvothermal treatment, which can improve their fluorescence response to Cu2+ ions [2]. For pf-CDs, the emergence of new peak at 3370 cm−1 indicates the introduction of OH groups, which can enhance their response to Fe3+ ions [17,34]. The peak at 2360 cm-1 in af- and pf-CDs may come from the carbon dioxide in the environment [26]. The above FT-IR spectra confirm the successful functionalization of CDs by selective introduction of functional groups, which can enhance the selectivity of CDs. The FT-IR spectra also indicate that CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements.

The optical absorption and fluorescent spectra of functionalized CDs are shown in Fig. 3 . Bare CNs exhibit very weak absorption in the whole UV–vis region. For the functionalized CDs, their absorption is evidently enhanced and redshifted, which may be attributed to the long-wavelength absorption of surface functional groups [35,36]. Meanwhile, the new peak of df-CDs at 300 nm is attributed to n-π* transition [37]. The excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of af-, df- and pf-CDs are shown in Fig. S3. Their optimal excitation wavelengths are located at 330, 390 and 330 nm, respectively, and their emission peaks are redshifted to 409, 500 and 452 nm, respectively. Due to the relatively strong absorption at 365 nm of af- and df-CDs, their fluorescence spectra were obtained by FS-365, and the fluorescence spectra of CNs and pf-CDs were detected by high powered FS-405. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence peaks of all samples are obviously red-shifted compared with the excitation wavelength. The fluorescent peaks of af- and df-CDs are at 465 nm, 499 nm and 586 nm, respectively, which are evidently shorter than that of CNs (520 nm) and pf-CDs (517 nm). Meanwhile, the stronger fluorescence of pf-CDs than CNs is realized by PEG-600 functionalization.

The fluorescent response of CNs and af-CDs to Cu2+ and Cr6+ ions is exhibited in Fig. 4 . To accurately estimate their linear response interval, its starting point should be at least three times greater than limit of detection (LOD) values. CNs exhibit weak fluorescence response to Cu2+ (narrow linear response interval of 20.0−80.0 μM and high LOD of 4954 nM at signal-to-noise ratio of 3). And af-CDs exhibit higher sensitivity for Cr6+, wider linear response interval of 0.8−80.0 μM and lower LOD of 242 nM. As shown in Fig. S4, df- and pf-CDs also exhibit obviously better fluorescence response to Cu2+ and Fe3+ (higher sensitivity, wider linear interval), respectively, and the specific parameters will be quantitatively compared in the following section.

Selectivity is one important parameter for CDs probes, so the effect of surface functionalization on response selectivity is especially focused on. As shown in Fig. 5 a, CNs exhibit low sensitivity and selectivity to Cu2+ due to their deficient functional groups. As a comparison, af-, df-, and pf-CDs exhibit significantly improved response to Cr6+, Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively, which should be due to the introduction of different functional groups during the hydrothermal process. It is worth noted that the response of pf-CDs to Fe2+ ions is negligible. Meanwhile, af-CDs exhibit weak response to some anions, as shown in Fig. S5, and the small error bars illustrate their good repeatability. In addition, the anti-interference results of these CDs are shown in Fig. S6, which indicate their good anti-interference ability.

To quantitatively compare the fluorescence response of four CDs probes, their response parameters are listed in Table 1 , which can intuitively indicate the enhanced fluorescence performance of CDs by the selective functionalization, and the following four points can be drawn: (1) the sensitivity of functionalized CD is more than 12 times that of CNs; (2) their selectivity is evidently improved from 0.3 of bare CNs to more than 2.0; (3) the functionalization treatment can significantly broaden the linear response interval of CDs to low-concentration region (i.e., 0.8–20.0 μM); (4) their LOD is significantly reduced from micromole to sub-micromole level.

Now the response speed and stability of functionalized CDs are also estimated, and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 6 , S7 and S8. After the addition of Cr6+, the fluorescence of af-CDs rapidly decays in the initial 1 min, and then remains stable within 40 min, which indicate their fast response and high stability. With NaCl increase from 10−5 to 1 M, the unchanged fluorescence intensity indicates their strong resistance to the ionic strength. af-CDs exhibit evidently weakened fluorescence under acidic and alkaline conditions, maybe due to the protonation or deprotonation of their functional groups under acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively, and CDs aggregation under alkaline ones also leads to fluorescence weakness [9,38,39]. af-CDs also exhibit strong pH dependent fluorescence response. Under acidic conditions, the existence of Cr6+ ions in the form of low-electron-density dichromate will weaken their interaction with CDs and corresponding deteriorating response of CDs probes [40]. Under alkaline conditions, Cr6+ ions mainly exist in the form of low oxidizing chromate, which is unfavorable for their redox reaction with af-CDs [38]. The similar results are obtained for df- and pf-CDs probes. So the fluorescence detection of functionalized CDs is performed under neutral conditions. As shown in Fig. S9 and Table S1, these CDs exhibit the good reversibility.

The response performance of our CDs is compared with some reference passivated/functionalized CDs sensors, as listed in Table 2 . For CDs-based Fe3+ sensors, surface passivation can enhance their response performance, but its low sensitivity and selectivity, narrow linear interval and low LOD hinder the practical application [9]. Many precursors, such as colistin [41], ionic liquid [17] and polyamine [42] are widely used to functionalize CDs for superior performance, but relatively low sensitivity and selectivity [41], high LOD [17] or narrow linear region [42] hinder their application. The similar results are also valid for Cu2+ and Cr6+ sensors [2,9,11,21,43–46]. In contrast, our functionalized CDs show relatively high sensitivity, good selectivity, wide response interval and low LOD for specific ions. Especially, this independent post-functionalization of CDs provides excellent solution to develop multifunctional CDs sensors.

The fluorescent response of functionalized CDs in natural lake water samples is shown in Table 3 . The measured ions concentration is almost equal to the added one and the recoveries are in the range of 94.0–102.5 %. In addition, the values of relative standard deviation (RSD) are defined as the ratio of the average and standard deviation of three independent measurements at each concentration, and below 8.1 %. These results indicate that our functionalized CDs are capable for simultaneous and highly selective ions detections in natural lake water.

Fluorescence quenching of CDs are usually classified as static quenching, dynamic quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) [30]. To judge the fluorescence quenching mechanism of our CDs, their temperature dependent fluorescence response curves were measured, and the results were shown in Fig. S10. With the increase of CDs solution temperature, their fluorescence quenching is evidently enhanced, indicating the dynamic quenching process of our CDs [47]. Due to the dominating role of functional groups in the fluorescence response of CDs, FT-IR technology can directly describe the interaction between CDs and the detected ions (Fig. S11). In the presence of Cr6+, the blue-shift of 1060 and 1570 cm−1 peaks and disappearance of 1410 cm−1 peak of af-CDs can be observed. Considering that the NH2 and COO groups of CNs exhibit neglected response to Cr6+, we think that the introduced CN bonds in ammonia functionalized CDs determine their response to Cr6+ [33]. For df-CDs, the addition of Cu2+ leads to their evident decrease and blue-shift of 1570 cm-1 peak (NH2), indicating that NH2 groups determine its fluorescence response to Cu2+ [2,7]. For pf-CDs, the presence of Fe3+ leads to the disappearance of 3370 cm−1 peak, indicating the determining role of the OH groups in their response to Fe3+ [48,49]. In a word, CN bonds, NH2 groups and OH groups are separately introduced into CDs in three hydrothermal process, and their selective interaction with three cations (i.e., Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+) leads to the selective response of CDs.

The fluorescence response of three functionalized CDs is shown in Scheme 1 . For af-CDs, the added Cr6+ ions can be preferentially combined with CN bonds [29,33], then the photo-generated electrons in CDs are easily transferred to Cr6+ ions and participate in their reduction into Cr3+ due to the redox potential of Cr6+/Cr3+ is more positive than conduction band of CDs [39,50]. This nonradiative electron consumption process leads to fluorescence quenching of af-CDs [51]. As shown in Fig. S12, the zeta potential of af-CDs/Cr6+ and af-CDs/Cr3+ system is -34.0 and -19.3 mV, respectively. After Cr6+ ions detection, the zeta potential of af-CDs/Cr6+ is raised up to -23.6 mV, which can further indicate the reduction of most of Cr6+ ions to Cr3+ by photogenerated electrons in af-CDs. Considering the overlap of Cr6+ absorption peak (i.e., 368 nm) and the excitation wavelength (i.e., 365 nm) of af-CDs (Fig. S13), the IFE effect of Cr6+ is one possible factor for the fluorescence quenching. While the absorption intensity of 80 μM Cr6+ solution accounts for merely 27 % of that of af-CDs, and the adjusted Cr6+ concentration is often far less than 80 μM during the whole experiment, so the IFE mechanism for af-CDs probes can be ignored. For df-CDs, the complexation of Cu2+ and their NH2 groups can promote d orbital separation of Cu2+, so a new electron channel is formed from excited state of df-CDs to the d orbital of Cu2+, then back to its ground state, which also leads to the fluorescence quenching of df-CDs [7]. pf-CDs exhibit response process similar to df-CDs besides the interaction between Fe3+ and their OH groups [48]. It should be particularly noted that different functional groups are easily introduced in clean CDs by the simple hydrothermal treatment, which lead to the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence of CDs probes.

In summary, clean CNs were successfully stripped from carbon rods by the electrochemical method. These CNs were converted into CDs less than 10 nm in size with narrow size distribution and simultaneously functionalized by ammonia, DMF and PEG-600 in the simple hydrothermal treatment process. FT-IR characterization demonstrates the introduction of different functional groups (i.e., CN, NH2 and OH) in af-, df- and pf-CDs, respectively. Compared with the weak fluorescence response of bare CNs, the sensitivity and selectivity of functionalized CDs can be enhanced by more than 12 and 7 times, respectively. Their LOD values are reduced by at least one order of magnitude, and the linear response intervals are significantly broadened to low-concentration region (i.e., 0.8–20.0 μM). Particularly this work provides the excellent feasibility of selective and controllable functionalization of CDs as fluorescent probes.

We have revised the Manuscript titled “Controllable functionalization of carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for independent Cr(Ⅵ), Fe(Ⅲ) and Cu(Ⅱ) ions detection” (ID: JPHOTOCHEM-d-20−01377) according to the reviewer’s comments. The relevant description has been marked with red font throughout the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by HIT(WH)-Weihai Co-construction Project (Grant No. ITGAZMZ001702), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2020MA083) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11674146).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113359.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and its application as a fluorescent probe for Cr(vi) ion detection
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Luo, P. Bai, X. Wang, G. Zhao, J. Feng, H. Ren, Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and its application as a fluorescent probe for Cr(vi) ion detection, New J. Chem. 43 (2019) 5488-5494.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A carbon-dot-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ions
ChemistrySelect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zheng, W. Liu, Q. Gai, Z, Tian, S. Ren, A carbon-dot-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ions, ChemistrySelect 4 (2019) 2392-2397.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient ratiometric fluorescence probe based on dual-emission quantum dots hybrid for on-site determination of copper ions
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yao, K. Zhang, H. Zhu, F. Ma, M. Sun, H. Yu, J. Sun, S. Wang, Efficient ratiometric fluorescence probe based on dual-emission quantum dots hybrid for on-site determination of copper ions, Anal. Chem. 85 (2013) 6461-6468.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemically diverse small molecule fluorescent chemosensors for copper ion
Coord. Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Sivaraman, M. Iniya, T. Anand, N. G. Kotla, O. Sunnapu, S. Singaravadivel, A. Gulyani, D. Chellappa, Chemically diverse small molecule fluorescent chemosensors for copper ion, Coord. Chem. Rev. 357 (2018) 50-104

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “Vigna radiata” based green C-dots: photo-triggered theranostics, fluorescent sensor for extracellular and intracellular iron (III) and multicolor live cell imaging probe
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

Mobin, “Vigna radiata” based green C-dots: photo-triggered theranostics, fluorescent sensor for extracellular and intracellular iron (III) and multicolor live cell imaging probe, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 291 (2019) 275-286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile hydrothermal method for preparation of fluorescent carbon dots on application of Fe3+ and fingerprint detection
Methods Appl. Fluoresc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Feng, Z. Yuan, T. Ren, A facile hydrothermal method for preparation of fluorescent carbon dots on application of Fe3+ and fingerprint detection, Methods Appl. Fluoresc. 7 (2019) 035001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots production via pyrolysis of sago waste as potential probe for metal ions sensing
J. Anal. Appl. Pyrolysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A universal facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots from cellulose-based biowaste for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions and intracellular bioimaging
Mater. Sci. Eng., C

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A green synthetic route for the surface-passivation of carbon dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescent sensor for the recognition and detection of toxic metal ions from aqueous solution
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Radhakrishnan, P. Panneerselvam, M. Marieeswaran, A green synthetic route for the surface-passivation of carbon dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescent sensor for the recognition and detection of toxic metal ions from aqueous solution, Anal. Methods 11 (2019) 490-506.
